g.data-narration: Welcome to Computer System Organization. This topic explores how hardware components work together to execute programs.
A computer has four structural components: CPU (brain), Main Memory (temporary storage), I/O Devices (communication), and System Bus (pathway).
The CPU has three main parts: Control Unit (coordinates activities), ALU (performs math/logic), and Registers (fast storage).
The CPU operates in a cycle: Fetch instruction from memory, Decode its meaning, Execute the operation, then update program counter.
Memory hierarchy balances speed, cost, and capacity: Registers (fastest), Cache, RAM, and Secondary Storage (slowest but largest).
Each memory type has unique characteristics: Registers are fastest but smallest, while secondary storage is slowest but holds terabytes of data.
I/O devices communicate with the CPU through three methods: Programmed I/O (CPU controls), Interrupt-driven I/O (device signals), and DMA (direct memory access).
Interrupts are signals requiring immediate attention: Device sends signal, CPU saves state, executes ISR (Interrupt Service Routine), then resumes execution.
Storage devices include magnetic disks (tracks, sectors, cylinders) and SSDs (flash memory with blocks and pages). Each has different access methods.
The boot process has six steps: Power-on, POST test, Bootstrap loader, Kernel loading, OS initialization, and User login.
Symmetric Multiprocessing (SMP) uses multiple CPUs sharing memory for increased throughput. Clustered systems connect multiple computers for reliability.
The Operating System manages hardware resources, allocates CPU time and memory, controls I/O operations, and provides user interface.
